evals.prompt: I need to calibrate 3 parameters for my phase-field simulation: thermal conductivity kappa (0.1-10 W/mK), mobility M (1e-6 to 1e-4 m2/s), and interface width W (0.5-2.0 um). I have a budget of 40 simulations. Can you generate a good sampling plan?
evals.expected_output: Agent generates a Latin Hypercube Sampling plan for 3 parameters with 40 samples using doe_generator.py, choosing LHS because dimension is moderate (3) and full coverage is not strictly needed.
assertions: Invokes scripts/doe_generator.py with --params 3 --budget 40 --method lhs --json
Selects LHS method based on decision guidance (3 params, general exploration)
Output includes samples, method, and coverage fields
Explains that LHS provides good space-filling coverage for 3D parameter spaces

evals.prompt: which parameters matter most? I got sensitivity scores: kappa=0.72, mobility=0.15, interface_width=0.08, dt=0.05
evals.expected_output: Agent runs sensitivity_summary.py and reports kappa as the dominant parameter with the highest influence, while dt has minimal impact.
assertions: Invokes scripts/sensitivity_summary.py with --scores 0.72,0.15,0.08,0.05 --names kappa,mobility,interface_width,dt --json
Output includes ranking field with parameters ordered by influence
Identifies kappa as the most influential parameter
Suggests focusing optimization effort on kappa and possibly mobility

evals.prompt: I have a 12-dimensional optimization problem with a budget of 200 evaluations. The simulation output is noisy (stochastic Monte Carlo). What optimizer should I use?
evals.expected_output: Agent recommends CMA-ES or an evolutionary algorithm due to high dimensionality, moderate budget, and high noise, using optimizer_selector.py.
assertions: Invokes scripts/optimizer_selector.py with --dim 12 --budget 200 --noise high --json
Output includes recommended optimizer, expected_evals, and notes
Recommends CMA-ES or evolutionary algorithm (not Bayesian Optimization, since dim > 10)
Mentions that high noise favors evolutionary or robust optimization methods

script_checks.description: Optimizer recommendation for 12D, budget 200, high noise

evals.prompt: Build me a surrogate from these data points. Inputs x: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 and outputs y: 2.1, 3.5, 4.2, 3.8, 2.6. Use RBF interpolation.
evals.expected_output: Agent fits an RBF surrogate model and returns honest fit-quality metrics. metrics.mse is the in-sample residual (near zero for the exact RBF interpolant) and metrics.cv_error is the leave-one-out cross-validation error used to judge true predictive quality; metrics.output_variance (~0.6024 for this data) is reported as a baseline only and is NOT labeled as the model error.
assertions: Invokes scripts/surrogate_builder.py with --x 0.1,0.3,0.5,0.7,0.9 --y 2.1,3.5,4.2,3.8,2.6 --model rbf --json
Output includes model_type, metrics, and notes fields, with metrics containing mse, cv_error, and output_variance
Reports model_type as rbf

metrics.mse reflects an actual fit residual: for the exact RBF interpolant it is ~0 (much smaller than output_variance ~0.6024), demonstrating the metric responds to fit quality rather than just echoing the data variance
Uses metrics.cv_error (leave-one-out, ~2.26 here) rather than the near-zero in-sample mse to characterize predictive quality, and does not present output_variance as the model error

script_checks.description: RBF surrogate fit with honest fit-quality metrics

evals.prompt: I only have 2 parameters (Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio) and I need to test every corner of the space. Generate a factorial design with 4 levels per parameter.
evals.expected_output: Agent generates a factorial DOE with 4^2=16 sample points covering all corners, selecting factorial method because dimension is low and full coverage is requested. The agent sets the level count explicitly, preferably via --levels 4 (which yields exactly 16 samples), or equivalently --budget 16 (the perfect power 4^2).
assertions: Invokes scripts/doe_generator.py with --params 2 --method factorial and sets 4 levels per parameter either via --levels 4 or via --budget 16, plus --json
Selects factorial method because dimension <= 3 and full coverage is needed
Output includes a samples array with exactly 16 points and coverage.levels == 4
Explains that factorial design gives all combinations for low-dimensional spaces and that 4 levels over 2 parameters yields 4^2 = 16 points

script_checks.description: Factorial DOE with 4 levels per param for 2 params (16 points)
